name: Essential Chinese
description: Mandarin from absolute zero, no character knowledge required. The tone and pinyin system comes first, then day-one survival phrases, then the highest-frequency words, then the grammar patterns that snap them together. Word cards show the characters big with tap-to-hear speech and a tap-to-reveal pinyin hint, so you try the characters first but are never stuck; the full pinyin appears with every answer, and the phrases you must be able to say are trained in both directions. Simplified characters. Keep new cards to 5-10 a day and say every answer out loud.

fields.front: 妈 mā, 麻 má, 马 mǎ, 骂 mà: same letters, four different words. What tells them apart?
fields.back: The tone: the pitch pattern of the syllable is part of the word itself.
fields.extra: mā mom, má hemp, mǎ horse, mà to scold. Mandarin has four tones plus a neutral tone; every pinyin syllable in this deck carries its tone mark.

fields.front: Tone 1, the flat bar: mā. How does it sound?
fields.back: High and level, like a sung note held steady.
fields.extra: Think of a doctor's 'say aaah'. Example: 妈 mā (mom).

fields.front: Tone 2, the rising accent: má. How does it sound?
fields.back: Rises from mid to high, like the end of an English question: What?
fields.extra: Example: 茶 chá (tea).

fields.front: Tone 3, the dipping accent: mǎ. How does it sound?
fields.back: Low and slow: it sinks to the bottom of your voice.
fields.extra: Often creaky, and it only rises again in slow, careful speech. Example: 水 shuǐ (water). Think low, not down-then-up.

fields.front: Tone 4, the falling accent: mà. How does it sound?
fields.back: Falls sharply from high to low, like a curt English 'No!'
fields.extra: Example: 是 shì (to be).

fields.front: The neutral tone: the second ma in māma. How does it sound?
fields.extra: It takes its pitch from the syllable before it and is written with no tone mark. Example: 谢谢 xièxie, second syllable.

fields.front: 你好 is written nǐ hǎo, two 3rd tones in a row. How is it actually pronounced?
fields.back: ní hǎo: when two 3rd tones meet, the first turns into a 2nd (rising) tone.
fields.extra: Automatic everywhere: 很好 hěn hǎo comes out hén hǎo. Pinyin keeps writing the original tones; your mouth makes the switch.

fields.front: Pinyin q, as in qǐng: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like English 'ch' but with lips spread wide and the tongue tip down behind the lower teeth.
fields.extra: 请 qǐng (please) sounds close to 'ching'. Never like the q in 'queen'.

fields.front: Pinyin x, as in xièxie: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like English 'sh' but with lips spread in a smile and the tongue tip down.
fields.extra: 谢谢 xièxie (thank you) sounds close to 'shyeh-shyeh'.

fields.front: Pinyin zh, as in zhè: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like the 'j' in judge, with the tongue curled back.
fields.extra: 这 zhè (this) sounds close to 'juh'.

fields.front: Pinyin c, as in cèsuǒ: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like the 'ts' in cats, at the start of the syllable.
fields.extra: 厕所 cèsuǒ (toilet) sounds close to 'tsuh-swaw'. Never like English k or s.

fields.front: Pinyin z, as in zàijiàn: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Like the 'ds' in kids: a quick 'dz'.
fields.extra: 再见 zàijiàn (goodbye) sounds close to 'dzai-jyen'.

fields.front: Pinyin r, as in rè: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Between an English 'r' and the 's' in measure, with no lip rounding.
fields.extra: 热 rè (hot). Keep your lips relaxed; English r rounds them.

fields.front: Pinyin ü, as in nǚ: how is it pronounced?
fields.back: Say 'ee', hold your tongue there, and round your lips.
fields.extra: 女 nǚ (female). After j, q, x, y it is written as plain u but still sounds like ü: 去 qù.

fields.front: Pinyin i after z, c, s, zh, ch, sh, r (as in sì and shí): how does it sound?
fields.back: Not 'ee': the vowel is a buzzed continuation of the consonant.
fields.extra: 四 sì (four) is close to 'szz', 十 shí (ten) to 'shrr'. Everywhere else i is 'ee': 你 nǐ.
Said ní hǎo: two 3rd tones in a row, so the first rises. 你 you + 好 good. Works with anyone, any time of day.
吗 turns a statement into a yes-no question. Standard reply: 我很好.
很 is required glue between a subject and an adjective; here it barely means 'very'.
Second syllable light and toneless. Add the person: 谢谢你.
Literally 'don't be polite'. 不 shifts to 2nd tone before a 4th tone.
The apology for real faults: bumping into someone, being late.

fields.meaning: excuse me, sorry to bother you
fields.extra: The everyday soft apology: squeezing past someone, interrupting, asking a favor. Real faults take 对不起.

fields.meaning: it doesn't matter (reply to an apology)
fields.extra: The standard answer to 对不起. Literally 'there is no connection'.
再 (again) + 见 (see): 'see you again'.
Starts polite requests: 请坐 (please sit). Also 'to treat': 我请你 (my treat).
The polite opener before any question to a stranger.
Answer with 我叫 wǒ jiào + your name.
Literally 'knowing you, (I'm) very glad'.
Country + 人 (person) = nationality. Swap in any country.
Literally 'I hear but can't understand'. The most useful sentence of your first month.
再 (again) + 说 (say) + 一遍 (once through).
Adjective + 一点 means 'a bit more so'.
会 marks a learned ability. 英语 English language.
Guaranteed smiles. 一点儿 a little.
Point and ask. Question words sit where the answer would go: 'this is what?'
多少 (how much) + 钱 (money). Point first: 这个多少钱？
The 太...了 wrapper means 'too/so'. Your opening move in any market haggle.
The follow-up to 太贵了. 便宜 cheap + 一点 a bit.
Point at the menu and say it; works in any shop or restaurant.

fields.meaning: I don't want it, no thanks
fields.extra: Firm but not rude; perfect for street vendors.
X 在哪儿 finds anything. Politer word for toilet: 卫生间 wèishēngjiān.
Call it out to get attention in a restaurant; normal, not rude. Literally 'service person'.
Say it to the server when you want to pay.
Literally 'dry the cup'. At formal toasts it can mean bottoms-up, so pace yourself.
等 (wait) + 一下 (a little); 一下 softens any verb.
The easygoing yes to any request. 问题 problem, question.
救 (save) + 命 (life). For everyday help say 请帮我 qǐng bāng wǒ.
Add 的 for 'my': 我的 wǒ de.
A polite form 您 nín exists, but 你 is fine in nearly every situation.
他 (he), 她 (she), and 它 (it) all sound identical; only the writing differs.
Same sound as 他; the woman radical 女 marks 'she' in writing only.
们 makes pronouns plural: 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
Also 'people'; a picture of a walking person. Country + 人 = nationality: 中国人.
男朋友 boyfriend and 女朋友 girlfriend add 男 (male) or 女 (female).
Takes a measure word before nouns: 这个 zhège (this one).
那个 nàge (that one). Don't confuse with 哪 nǎ (which), which has an extra mouth 口.
Fills empty slots when reading numbers aloud: room 302 reads sān líng èr.
Plain yī when counting; inside phrases the tone shifts (see the tone-change cards).
Two OF something uses 两 liǎng instead: 两个人.
三点 (3 o'clock), 三个人 (three people).
Unlucky: sounds like 死 sǐ (death); buildings often skip the 4th floor. Mind sì (4) vs shí (10).
五块钱 (five yuan).
A lucky number; 666 in Chinese chat means 'awesome', nothing sinister.
七点 (7 o'clock).
The lucky number: it sounds like 发 fā (to get rich). Plates full of 8s cost extra.
Sounds like 久 jiǔ (long-lasting), so nine is auspicious for anniversaries.
Buzzed vowel: 'shrr'. sì (4) falls, shí (10) rises.
一百 (100), 三百 (300). 105 is 一百零五, with 零 filling the empty tens slot.
多少钱？ (how much?). The metal radical 钅 recalls coins.

fields.meaning: two of something (before a measure word)
fields.extra: 两个人 (two people), never 二个人. 二 counts in the abstract; 两 counts things.

fields.text: Numbers 11 to 19 stack ten + digit: 11 is {{c1::十一 shíyī}}, 14 is {{c2::十四 shísì}}.
fields.extra: The same pattern runs through 十九 (19).

fields.text: Tens stack digit + ten: 20 is {{c1::二十 èrshí}}, 99 is {{c2::九十九 jiǔshíjiǔ}}.
fields.extra: Mix freely: 25 is 二十五 èrshíwǔ.

fields.front: 一 yī changes tone in phrases. Before a 4th-tone syllable, like 下 xià, how is it said?
fields.extra: Same spirit as 不 turning into bú before 4th tones. Pinyin in this deck writes the spoken tone.

fields.front: 一 yī changes tone in phrases. Before a 1st, 2nd, or 3rd tone syllable, like 点 diǎn, how is it said?
fields.extra: Plain yī survives only when counting or reading out digits.
Sits where the answer would go: 这是什么？ ('this is what?')
Formal reading shuí. 他是谁？ (Who is he?)
你在哪儿？ (Where are you?). The South and Taiwan say 哪里 nǎlǐ instead.
Literally 'many-few'. No measure word needed: 多少钱？ For small counts use 几 jǐ.

fields.meaning: how is it?, how about it?
fields.extra: The all-purpose 'what do you think': 这个怎么样？ (How about this one?)
哪个 (which one), 你是哪国人？ (Which country are you from?). It is 那 (that) plus a mouth 口 asking the question.
For answers under about ten, always with a measure word: 几个人？ Also 几点？ (what time?)
怎么说？ (How do you say it?), 怎么去？ (How do I get there?)
Toneless. Statement + 吗 = question: 你是老师吗？ Word order never changes.
Returns a question: 我很好，你呢？ (I'm fine, and you?)
Links nouns only: 我是学生 (I am a student). Never before adjectives: 我很好, not 我是好.
Also 'there is'. Negated with 没, never 不: 没有. 有问题吗？ (Any questions?)
我要这个 (I want this one). Direct; for 'would like to do' use 想.
想 + verb: 我想去 (I'd like to go), softer than 要. With a person it means to miss: 我想你 (I miss you).
去 + place: 去中国 (go to China).
Also 'bring me' when ordering: 来一杯茶 (a cup of tea, please).
吃饭 (have a meal). 你吃了吗？ (Have you eaten?) doubles as a friendly greeting.
喝水 (drink water), 喝茶 (drink tea). The mouth radical 口 marks a mouth action.
买东西 (go shopping). 卖 mài (to sell) differs by tone and a top stroke: 3rd tone buys, 4th tone sells.
Also to read: 看书 (read a book), 看电影 (watch a movie). A hand 手 shading an eye 目.
Also to hear: 听不懂 (hear but not understand) is the one you will use most.
说汉语 (speak Chinese). 说话 means to talk in general.
我会说汉语 (I can speak Chinese). Compare 能 néng: can in the moment.
Ability or permission in the moment: 我不能去 (I can't go). Learned skills take 会.
我在家 (I'm at home). Before a verb it marks an ongoing action: 我在吃饭 (I'm eating).
你住在哪儿？ (Where do you live?). Hotels too: 住饭店.
Also negates completed actions: 我没有去 (I didn't go). Often shortened to 没.
做饭 (cook). Same sound as 坐 (to sit); context decides.
请坐 (please sit). Also to take transport: 坐飞机 (take a plane), 坐出租车 (take a taxi). Two people 人 sitting on earth 土.
我叫 + your name (My name is...). Also to summon: 叫出租车 (call a taxi).
我学习汉语 (I study Chinese). 学 alone works too.
Also the noun 'job': 我在中国工作 (I work in China); 你的工作 (your job).
我要睡觉 (I want to sleep). Not 水饺 shuǐjiǎo (boiled dumplings); tones matter.
打 (to hit) is the verb for calls: 给我打电话 (call me). 电话 is 'electric speech'.
我爱你 (I love you). Strong; for everyday liking use 喜欢.
我喜欢中国菜 (I like Chinese food). Takes nouns or verbs: 喜欢看书.
天 (day) builds the whole trio: 今天, 明天, 昨天.
明 (bright: sun 日 + moon 月). 明天见 (see you tomorrow).
昨 carries the sun radical 日: a day already passed.
现在几点？ (What time is it now?)
Answer: number + 点: 三点 (3 o'clock).
三点 (3 o'clock). Also 'to order food' (点菜) and 'a little' (一点).
Dates run big to small: 2026年7月9日. 新年 (New Year).
Also 'moon'; the character draws a crescent. Months are just numbers: 一月 (January) through 十二月 (December).
Weekdays count up: 星期一 (Monday) to 星期六 (Saturday); Sunday is 星期天.
上 (upper) + 午 (noon). The afternoon is 下午.
下 (lower) + 午 (noon): the part of the day below noon.
Second syllable is neutral tone.
Woman radical 女 plus 马 mǎ (horse) lending the sound.
子 here is a neutral-tone noun suffix, the same one as in 杯子 (cup).
女 (female) + 儿 (child). The apostrophe separates the syllables; ü keeps its dots after n.
老 (old, venerable) + 师 (master). Address teachers as surname + 老师: 王老师.
学 (to study) + 生 (person). 我是学生 (I am a student).
医 (medicine) + 生 (person). 医院 (hospital) shares the same 医.
Surname first: 王先生 (Mr. Wang). Literally 'born earlier'.
Fine after a surname: 王小姐. Alone to a stranger it can offend; say 服务员 to staff instead.
热水 (hot water) is the default drink offered in China.
Both 'chai' and 'cha' around the world come from this word.
A loanword from 'coffee'. 一杯咖啡 (a cup of coffee).
米 (raw rice) + 饭 (cooked food, meal). 吃饭 (have a meal) uses the same 饭.
Also 'vegetable'. 中国菜 (Chinese food), 点菜 (order dishes). The grass radical 艹 hints at its vegetable origin.
水 (water) + 果 (fruit): juicy produce.
Also the brand: 苹果手机 (iPhone).
杯 doubles as the measure word for drinks. Same 杯 as in 干杯 (cheers).
Also 'family'. A pig 豕 under a roof 宀: the ancient picture of a household. 回家 (go home).
中 (middle) + 国 (country): the Middle Kingdom. 中国人, 中国菜.
美 (beautiful) + 国 (country), picked to echo the 'me' in America. 美国人 (American).
汉 (the Han people) + 语 (language). You will also hear 中文 Zhōngwén; both are fine.
出租 (to rent out) + 车 (vehicle). 坐出租车 (take a taxi); in speech also 打车 dǎchē.
手 (hand) + 机 (machine). 我的手机 (my phone).
学 (study) + 校 (school building). 去学校 (go to school).
饭 (meal) + 店 (shop). In big cities a 饭店 is often a hotel; a small eatery is a 饭馆.
商 (commerce) + 店 (shop). 去商店买东西 (go to the store to buy things).
医 (medicine) + 院 (institution).
看书 (read), 一本书 (one book).
Literally 'east-west'. 买东西 (buy things, go shopping).
飞 (to fly) + 机 (machine). The airport, 机场 jīchǎng, shares the 机.
Literally 'electric brain'. 电 also starts 电话 (phone) and 电影 (movie).
买衣服 (buy clothes).
Animal radical 犭 on the left. 小狗 (puppy).
饭店在前面 (the restaurant is up ahead).
在学校后面 (behind the school). Opposite of 前面.
Follows the noun: 家里 (at home), 学校里 (inside the school).
Woman 女 + child 子 = good. Also an agreeable 'OK' on its own: 好的.
A person with arms stretched wide. 大学 (university, literally 'big school').
小狗 (puppy). 小 + surname is a friendly address for someone younger: 小王.
太贵了 (too expensive).
便宜一点 (a little cheaper). Opposite of 贵.
很多 (a lot). Opposite: 少 shǎo (few). 多少 (how much) stacks them.
今天很冷 (it's cold today). The two-stroke ice radical 冫 sits on the left.
热水 (hot water). The four dots at the bottom are the fire radical.
高 (high) + 兴 (spirits). 认识你很高兴 (nice to meet you).
The liang is a neutral tone. Works for people, clothes, and cities alike.
Toneless. 我的手机 (my phone), 你的书 (your book). The most common character in Chinese.
After a verb: completed (我买了 I bought it). At sentence end: new situation (下雨了 it's raining now).
Required between subject and adjective: 他很好. Often so weak it just means 'is'.
The all-purpose negator: 不是, 不去. Shifts to bú before 4th tones: 不是 bú shì. 有 takes 没 instead.
The 太...了 pattern: 太贵了 (too expensive), 太好了 (great!).
Goes after the subject, before the verb: 我们都去 (we are all going).
我和你 (you and I). It cannot join sentences the way English 'and' does.
一点儿水 (a little water). After adjectives: 便宜一点儿 (a bit cheaper).
个 gè is the default measure word. Number + measure word + noun is mandatory: never 一人.
本 běn is the measure word for books and notebooks.
杯 bēi measures drinks: 一杯咖啡, 一杯水.
块 kuài is the spoken unit of money (the formal word is 元 yuán); 钱 often drops off: 十块.
Age needs no verb. 岁 suì (years old). Ask a child 你几岁？, an adult 你多大？

fields.text: Ask a yes-no question: 你去{{c1::吗}}？nǐ qù {{c1::ma}}? (Are you going?)
fields.extra: 吗 turns any statement into a question; the word order never changes.

fields.text: Bounce a question back: 我很好，你{{c1::呢}}？wǒ hěn hǎo, nǐ {{c1::ne}}? (I'm fine, and you?)
Make it yours: 我{{c1::的}}手机 wǒ {{c1::de}} shǒujī (my phone)
fields.extra: 的 links owner to thing: 你的书 (your book).

fields.text: Say no: 我{{c1::不}}去 wǒ {{c1::bú}} qù (I'm not going)
fields.extra: 不 negates most verbs and adjectives. It reads bú here because 去 is a 4th tone.

fields.text: The exception: 我{{c1::没}}有钱 wǒ {{c1::méi}}yǒu qián (I don't have money)
fields.extra: 有 is always negated with 没, never 不.

fields.text: Adjective glue: 他{{c1::很}}好 tā {{c1::hěn}} hǎo (He is fine)
fields.extra: Subject + 很 + adjective; no 是.
The same frame as 太贵了 (too expensive).

fields.text: All of us: 我们{{c1::都}}去 wǒmen {{c1::dōu}} qù (We are all going)
fields.extra: 都 sits after the subject, before the verb.

fields.text: You and me: 我{{c1::和}}你 wǒ {{c1::hé}} nǐ (me and you)
fields.extra: 和 joins nouns only.

fields.text: Say where you are: 我{{c1::在}}家 wǒ {{c1::zài}} jiā (I'm at home)
fields.extra: 在 + place; no extra 'is' needed.

fields.text: Mark it done: 我买{{c1::了}} wǒ mǎi {{c1::le}} (I bought it)
fields.extra: 了 after the verb marks a completed action.

fields.text: Count with a measure word: 三{{c1::个}}人 sān {{c1::ge}} rén (three people)
fields.extra: Number + measure word + noun, always.

fields.text: Learned skills: 我{{c1::会}}说汉语 wǒ {{c1::huì}} shuō Hànyǔ (I can speak Chinese)
fields.extra: In-the-moment ability would take 能 instead.

fields.text: Find anything: 医院在{{c1::哪儿}}？yīyuàn zài {{c1::nǎr}}? (Where is the hospital?)
fields.extra: Swap in any place: 厕所在哪儿？

fields.text: Order anything: 我{{c1::要}}一杯咖啡 wǒ {{c1::yào}} yì bēi kāfēi (I want a cup of coffee)
